attributes.stem:
A 55-year-old man with documented chronic gastro-oesophageal reflux presents for follow-up management of his symptoms. He has been experiencing epigastric pain which radiates retrosternally. Up until recently he had been able to manage his symptoms with a combination of antacids and intermittent use of ranitidine. An endoscopy has shown erosive oesophagitis, but no evidence of Barrett's oesophagus. Three months ago his gastroenterologist started him on omeprazole and this has provided good symptomatic relief. The patient is otherwise in good health.

Since then he has been reading about proton-pump inhibitors and understands that the long term use of these agents could have their own risks. He is aware that there are many potential side effects and long-term effects of these medications and asks the doctor if there is any good evidence of detrimental effects from their long-term use.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate advice?
options: Increased risk of Clostridioides difficile infections
Increased risk of community acquired pneumonia
attributes.explanation:
Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) are widely used for peptic ulcer disease, gastritis, and erosive oesophagitis. They work by inhibiting the H+/K+ ATPase pump in the gastric parietal cells, hence decreasing gastric acid production.

The literature contains reports of many of the potential risks, such bone fracture, acute and chronic renal disease, gastrointestinal infections and magnesium deficiency, but most of these studies involve low-quality data (case-control and/or observational studies) and are subject to bias. Many studies have shown a significantly increased risk of Clostridioides difficile infection in patients who are taking PPIs (eg, omeprazole). Cl difficile infection presents with watery diarrhoea, nausea, and abdominal pain. Although Cl difficile infection is more commonly associated with the use of antibiotics, the risk stays elevated in patients on PPI therapy even without the use of antibiotics. However, all these studies have been observational, retrospective, non-randomised, and suffer from significant heterogeneity.

An observational study from Korea found an increased incidence of gastric cancer in PPI users, where the association was dose and time-dependent. These findings do suggest a warning with regards the use of high dose PPIs in patients predisposed to develop cancer, such as after H. pylori eradication or after identification of asymptomatic Barrett’s oesophagus.

There have been a number of observational studies suggesting a small association between PPIs and community-acquired pneumonia. However these studies all had limitations including unmeasured confounders, such existing co-morbidities in the studied populations.

The information in the literature regarding any association between PPI-use and cognitive decline is inconsistent and conflicting. An overall perspective would suggest a lack of any association between PPI use and changes in cognitive function. Further studies in this area are required to see if there is any correlation between long term PPI use and cognitive decline.

Although PPIs may interfere with vitamin absorption in the gut, clinically significant vitamin deficiency is rarely seen in patients with no malabsorption syndromes. Hence, current guidelines do not recommend supplementation or screening for vitamin deficiencies in patients taking PPIs. Whilst there have been reports of spinal and hip fractures in patients using PPIs, such therapy appears to have little to no effect on bone mineral density. This suggests that if there is any association between PPI use and fractures it is not likely to be related to osteoporosis.

Proton pump inhibitor use has been reported as a rare cause of acute interstitial nephritis. This is the result of a reaction to the drug, not its long-term use. PPIs have also been implicated in cases of iron and magnesium deficiency, but the reported data is low quality.

Whilst all these potential risks for the long-term use of proton pump need to be considered, there are no current high-quality studies giving a clear picture to any particular adverse event. Thus the most appropriate advice to give a patient using a proton pump inhibitor is that there are no proven serious adverse effects with regards to long-term use. However, their long-term use should be periodically assessed.
learningPoints: Proton pump inhibitors have their theoretical risks, but most studies do not provide clear-cut documented evidence.
references: Schubert ML. Proton pump inhibitors: placing putative adverse effects in proper perspective. Curr Opin Gastroenterol 2019, 35:509–16.
Jenkins D, Modolell I. Proton pump inhibitors. BMJ 2023; 383:e070752
Khan Z, Mehan S, Saifi, MA, et al. Proton Pump Inhibitors and Cognitive Health: Review on Unraveling the Dementia Connection and Co-morbid Risks.  Curr Alzheimer Res. 2024;20(11):739-757.
Thurber KM, Otto AO, Stricker SL. Proton pump inhibitors: Understanding the associated risks and benefits of long-term use. Am J Health Syst Pharm. 2023; 80:487-94.
